tree_title: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
overall_topic: Understanding USAG Gymnastics Levels: A Complete Guide (2024)
questions: Identify the Long Island–based gymnastics organization that provides structured training aligned with the USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system—from developmental through Elite—and encourages parents to have their athletes evaluated for appropriate placement. Using information from both (1) a parent-focused article published by this organization about supporting a young gymnast’s competitive journey and (2) the dedicated webpage for its Smithtown, NY facility, explain the purpose and intended audience of the parent-focused guide, and then detail the Smithtown location’s address, contact information, hours of operation, range of programs offered, coaching leadership background, and the total number of locations the organization operates.

A well-known gymnastics training organization in the Northeastern United States supports athletes progressing through the USA Gymnastics (USAG) Development Program levels and also provides structured competitive pathways beyond the compulsory and optional stages. Identify the organization’s Long Island location situated in Suffolk County and provide its full street address, phone number, email, and regular weekday operating hours. Then, summarize the structure and training commitments of its three distinct team pathways (including how often athletes typically train and the level of travel or competitive scope involved). Finally, explain how this organization positions itself as a resource for parents navigating the USAG level system and competitive progression for their children.

A U.S.-based gymnastics training center publishes several in-depth parent-focused guides explaining its programs and athlete development pathway. Identify four distinct informational guides from the same provider that respectively: (1) explain how enrolling young children in gymnastics supports early physical, social, and emotional development; (2) break down the 2024 USA Gymnastics (USAG) level structure from developmental levels through Elite; (3) describe the rapid growth and benefits of youth Ninja-style training classes; and (4) offer advice to parents supporting a child through the competitive gymnastics journey. For each guide, summarize the central theme and outline at least two specific benefits, features, or structural details highlighted in the content.

Identify a set of informational resources published by a U.S.-based gymnastics training center that collectively guide families from early childhood participation through the competitive level system and into advanced training pathways. These resources include: (1) an overview of the USA Gymnastics level structure for 2024, (2) a discussion of how early gymnastics participation benefits children’s physical, social, and emotional development, (3) an article explaining the growth and benefits of youth Ninja-style gymnastics classes, and (4) a guide written for parents supporting athletes in competitive gymnastics. For each resource, summarize its primary focus, the specific benefits or skills it highlights (physical, mental, social, or competitive), and explain how, when considered together, they present a comprehensive developmental pathway from beginner or recreational participation to high-level competitive or elite gymnastics.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: As a parent deeply involved in the gymnastics community, you already appreciate the blend of strength, flexibility, and mental resilience required in this demanding sport. Thi
rounds.answer_a:
The Long Island–based organization is Gold Medal Gymnastics Center (GMGC), which provides structured training through the USA Gymnastics (USAG) system and offers evaluations for athlete placement [2].

GMGC's parent-focused guide, "A Parent's Guide to Your Child's Competitive Journey," is intended for parents of gymnasts entering or currently in the competitive program. Its purpose is to educate these parents on what to expect, defining the roles of the athlete, coach, and parent. The guide offers advice on supporting a child through the highs and lows of competition, managing time commitments, and fostering a positive environment, thereby preparing families for the demands of the sport [1].

The GMGC facility in Smithtown is one of seven locations the organization operates. The coaching leadership includes owner Mihai "Misha" Gelen, a former Romanian national champion and collegiate coach, and Boys Team Director Alexandru "Sasha" Rico [2].

**Smithtown, NY Location Details:**
*   **Address:** 253 W. Main Street, Smithtown, NY 11787 [2].
*   **Contact:** Phone: (631) 366-4653; Email: smithtown@gmgc.com [2].
*   **Hours of Operation:** Monday–Friday, 9:00 AM – 8:30 PM; Saturday, 9:00 AM – 1:00 PM; Sunday, 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM [2].
*   **Programs Offered:** The facility provides a range of programs including Preschool, Boys & Girls Recreational classes, Tumbling, and Ninja classes. It is also a host site for GMGC’s competitive teams, which follow the USAG Development Program (Levels 1-10) and Xcel Program [2].

## References
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (2020, December 1). *A Parent's Guide to Your Child's Competitive Journey*. [Blog post]. Retrieved from gmgc.com.
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (n.d.). *Smithtown*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/locations/smithtown.
rounds.answer_b:
The organization is **Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers**, a Long Island–based program that explicitly uses the **USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system**, from developmental levels through **Elite**, and tells parents to have athletes evaluated for proper placement in the appropriate class or team track [1][2].  

Its parent-focused article, **“A Parent’s Guide to Supporting Your Child’s Gymnastics Dreams,”** is intended for **parents of young gymnasts entering or progressing through competitive gymnastics**. The guide’s purpose is to help families understand how to support a child’s competitive journey: fostering healthy motivation, balancing school and training, promoting nutrition, rest, and mental well-being, communicating well with coaches, and keeping the experience positive and developmentally appropriate [1].  

The organization’s **Smithtown, NY** facility is located at **8 Dauphin Street, Smithtown, NY 11787**. Contact details listed for that location are **(631) 360-0400** and **smithtown@goldmedalgymnastics.com** [2]. Its hours are **Monday–Friday 9:00 a.m.–8:00 p.m., Saturday 9:00 a.m.–3:00 p.m., and Sunday closed** [2]. Programs at Smithtown include **instructional gymnastics**, **boys’ and girls’ competitive team**, **preschool and developmental classes**, **tumbling**, **ninja**, **birthday parties/camps/events**, and other age-appropriate recreational-to-competitive offerings [2].  

The Smithtown page says the coaching leadership includes an experienced staff and identifies **office/administrative leadership and coaching personnel**, with the broader organization emphasizing professionally trained coaches working within the USAG pathway [2][3]. Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers operates **four Long Island locations**: **Centereach, Smithtown, Huntington, and East Northport** [2][3].

## References
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “A Parent’s Guide to Supporting Your Child’s Gymnastics Dreams.”  
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “Smithtown” location webpage.  
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, main site/program information and locations pages.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the core entity (Gold Medal Gymnastics) and the Smithtown, NY facility, so both pass the DEEP logic check. However, Agent B fails the WIDTH/Completeness check with multiple major factual inaccuracies: incorrect Smithtown address (not 253 West Main Street), incorrect phone number, incorrect hours, and an incorrect statement that the organization operates four locations instead of seven. These are significant checklist failures. Agent A provides the correct address, contact information, hours, program summary, and correctly states that the organization operates seven locations. While Agent A’s leadership description does not fully reflect the expected high-level USSR/Master of Sport background detail, it is a minor issue compared to Agent B’s numerous concrete errors. In terms of presentation, both are well-structured, but Agent A’s information is substantially more accurate and aligned with the ground truth. Therefore, Agent A provides the better overall user experience.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: As a parent deeply involved in the gymnastics community, you already appreciate the blend of strength, flexibility, and mental resilience required in this demanding sport. Thi
rounds.answer_a:
The gymnastics organization with a location in Suffolk County, Long Island is the Gold Medal Gymnastics Center (GMGC). Its facility in the area is located at **253 W. Main Street, Smithtown, NY 11787**. Contact information includes the phone number **(631) 366-4653** and the email **smithtown@gmgc.com**. Regular weekday operating hours are Monday through Friday, from 9:00 AM to 8:30 PM [1].

GMGC offers three distinct competitive team pathways structured to accommodate different levels of commitment and ambition [2].

1.  **Pre-Team:** This developmental program serves as the entry point to competitive gymnastics. It focuses on building the foundational strength, flexibility, and skills required for higher levels, offering an introduction to the competitive environment with a lower time commitment [2].

2.  **USAG Development Program (DP):** This is a rigorous pathway designed for dedicated athletes aiming for collegiate or elite-level gymnastics. It follows the USAG structure from compulsory (Levels 1-5) to optional (Levels 6-10), with training that can reach up to 25 hours per week. This track involves significant dedication and requires travel for state, regional, and national competitions [2].

3.  **USAG Xcel Program:** This alternative competitive track provides more flexibility for gymnasts who want to compete without the intensive time commitment of the DP track. It allows athletes to pursue other activities while still training and competing. The Xcel program has its own progressive levels (Bronze to Sapphire) and is geared toward a less demanding schedule [2].

GMGC positions itself as a key resource for parents by providing educational content to help them navigate the USAG system. The organization published "A Parent's Guide to Your Child's Competitive Journey," an article designed to prepare parents for the demands of the sport. It defines the roles of the parent, athlete, and coach, and offers strategies for supporting a child through the process. By creating such guides, GMGC proactively demystifies the competitive progression, empowering parents to make informed decisions and foster a positive athletic environment for their children [3].

## References
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (n.d.). *Smithtown*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/locations/smithtown.
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (n.d.). *Competitive Team*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/programs/competitive-team.
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (2020, December 1). *A Parent's Guide to Your Child's Competitive Journey*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/blog/a-parents-guide-to-your-childs-competitive-journey.
rounds.answer_b:
The Suffolk County Long Island location is **Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers – Smithtown**, located at **8 Dauphin Street, Smithtown, NY 11787**. Its listed contact information is **(631) 360-0400** and **smithtown@goldmedalgymnastics.com**. Regular weekday hours are **Monday through Friday, 9:00 a.m. to 8:00 p.m.** [1]

Gold Medal presents **three team pathways** for athletes advancing through the USAG system and beyond. The **Developmental Team** is designed for younger or emerging competitors preparing for the formal competitive pipeline; it builds foundational skills, strength, and discipline before full compulsory/optional progression [2]. The **Girls Competitive Team** follows the USAG Development Program structure, moving from compulsory and optional levels toward higher-level competition. Training intensifies as athletes progress, with team members practicing multiple days per week and competing in a broader meet schedule that can extend beyond local competition [2][3]. The **Elite Team** is the highest-performance track, intended for gymnasts pursuing advanced USAG and elite-level goals. This pathway requires the greatest training commitment, with significantly more hours in the gym and a wider competitive scope, including higher-level regional and national-caliber opportunities [2][3].

For parents, Gold Medal explicitly positions itself as a guide to the competitive gymnastics journey. Its parent-focused article, **“A Parent’s Guide to Supporting Your Child’s Gymnastics Dreams,”** is written to help families understand training progression, motivation, communication with coaches, and the physical and emotional demands of the sport [4]. Across its program materials, the organization also encourages parents to have athletes **evaluated for proper placement** within the USAG-based system, reinforcing its role as both a training center and an educational resource for navigating developmental, competitive, and elite pathways [2][3][4].

## References
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “Smithtown” location page, https://goldmedalgymnasticscenter.com/locations/smithtown/  
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, team/program information pages, https://goldmedalgymnasticscenter.com/  
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, competitive program descriptions and USAG pathway information, official site.  
[4] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “A Parent’s Guide to Supporting Your Child’s Gymnastics Dreams,” official site.

First, Deep Logic: Agent A identifies the correct organization name but provides incorrect location details (wrong street address, phone number, and email domain), which fails the entity verification requirement. Agent B correctly identifies the Smithtown (Suffolk County) location at 8 Dauphin Street with matching contact details, satisfying Deep Logic.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A misses required program distinctions (does not identify Club Team, USAIGC, and Junior Olympic/USAG Development Program as specified) and includes incorrect pathways (Pre-Team, Xcel). Agent B also does not accurately describe the three required pathways (Club Team, USAIGC, Junior Olympic/USAG DP), instead listing Developmental, Girls Competitive, and Elite Teams—so B has checklist gaps as well. However, B correctly provides the required contact details and weekday hours, while A’s are inaccurate.

Finally, User Experience: Agent B is clearer, more structured, and factually stronger on core location data. Although imperfect on pathway specificity, it better satisfies the primary identification and contact requirements. Agent A contains significant factual inaccuracies, making it less trustworthy overall.

Therefore, Agent B is better, but not MUCH_BETTER due to its incomplete pathway accuracy.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Ever wondered how early gymnastics can shape your child’s future? Signing your little one up for gymnastics classes is more than just physical fun. It’s a chance to grow their
rounds.answer_a:
Gold Medal Gymnastics Center (GMGC) publishes several parent-focused guides covering athlete development and its various programs.

1.  **"Building Blocks for Life: Why Gymnastics is the Best Sport for Young Children"**: This guide’s central theme is that gymnastics provides a foundational experience for early childhood development. It details physical benefits such as improved coordination, balance, and kinesthetic awareness, which it calls the "ABCs of athleticism." The guide also highlights social and emotional gains, explaining that structured classes teach children to listen, follow a coach’s directions, take turns, and build confidence through mastering new skills [1].

2.  **"USA Gymnastics Levels Explained 2024: A Parent’s Guide"**: This resource breaks down the complex USA Gymnastics (USAG) level system for parents. Its main purpose is to clarify the different competitive pathways. The guide explains the structure of the Development Program (DP), a linear progression from Level 1 through 10 designed for highly dedicated athletes. It also describes the separate, more flexible Xcel Program and the pinnacle Elite Program for gymnasts with international competitive aspirations [2].

3.  **"The Rise of Ninja: More Than Just a Class, It’s a Movement"**: This article explains the growing popularity and benefits of Ninja-style training programs. The central theme is that Ninja classes offer a unique, hybrid athletic experience by combining elements from gymnastics, martial arts, and obstacle course training. Two key features it promotes are the development of "athletic intelligence" through obstacle navigation and the way the program builds discipline and confidence in a high-energy, motivating format [3].

4.  **"A Parent's Guide to Your Child's Competitive Journey"**: This guide offers advice to parents whose children are entering or are in the competitive program. The theme is preparing families for the sport's demands and fostering a healthy support system. It emphasizes the importance of the "Trifecta," defining the distinct roles of the parent, athlete, and coach. The content offers specific strategies for managing the significant time commitment and helping a child handle the emotional ups and downs of competition [4].

## References
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (2024, May 1). *Building Blocks for Life: Why Gymnastics is the Best Sport for Young Children*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/blog/building-blocks-for-life-why-gymnastics-is-the-best-sport-for-young-children.
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (2024, April 1). *USA Gymnastics Levels Explained 2024: A Parent’s Guide*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/blog/usa-gymnastics-levels-explained-2024-a-parents-guide.
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (2024, February 1). *The Rise of Ninja: More Than Just a Class, It’s a Movement*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/blog/the-rise-of-ninja-more-than-just-a-class-its-a-movement.
[4] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (2020, December 1). *A Parent's Guide to Your Child's Competitive Journey*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/blog/a-parents-guide-to-your-childs-competitive-journey.
rounds.answer_b:
The provider is **Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers**, which publishes multiple parent-oriented guides explaining youth development, program pathways, and competitive progression [1][2][3][4].

**1) Early childhood gymnastics guide:** In **“The Benefits of Enrolling Your Child in Gymnastics Classes at a Young Age,”** the central theme is that starting gymnastics early supports whole-child development, not just athletic ability [1]. The article highlights physical benefits such as improved **strength, flexibility, coordination, and balance** [1]. It also emphasizes social-emotional gains, including **confidence, discipline, listening skills, and social interaction** as children learn in a structured group setting [1].

**2) 2024 USAG levels guide:** In **“Understanding the 2024 USAG Levels in Gymnastics,”** Gold Medal explains the competitive pathway from **developmental levels through Elite**, helping parents understand how gymnasts progress in the sport [2]. Structural details include the distinction between **developmental/introductory stages and the formal USA Gymnastics level system**, as well as progression through **compulsory and optional levels toward Elite** [2]. The guide also notes that placement depends on **skill mastery and evaluation**, helping families see how advancement decisions are made [2].

**3) Ninja training guide:** In **“The Rise of Ninja Classes: Why Kids Love This New Fitness Trend,”** the main theme is the growing popularity of Ninja-style training as a fun, high-energy alternative or complement to traditional gymnastics [3]. The article highlights benefits such as improved **agility, strength, coordination, and obstacle-navigation skills** [3]. It also stresses that Ninja classes are appealing because they are **engaging, confidence-building, and suitable for kids who enjoy movement challenges in a less traditional format** [3].

**4) Competitive-parent guide:** In **“A Parent’s Guide to Supporting Your Child’s Gymnastics Dreams,”** the focus is helping parents navigate the demands of a young gymnast’s competitive journey [4]. It advises parents to support **healthy motivation, communication with coaches, and realistic expectations** [4]. Additional features include guidance on balancing **training with school and family life**, while also prioritizing **nutrition, rest, and emotional well-being** so the experience remains positive and sustainable [4].

## References
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “The Benefits of Enrolling Your Child in Gymnastics Classes at a Young Age.” https://goldmedalgymnasticscenter.com/the-benefits-of-enrolling-your-child-in-gymnastics-classes-at-a-young-age/  
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “Understanding the 2024 USAG Levels in Gymnastics.” https://goldmedalgymnasticscenter.com/understanding-the-2024-usag-levels-in-gymnastics/  
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “The Rise of Ninja Classes: Why Kids Love This New Fitness Trend.” https://goldmedalgymnasticscenter.com/the-rise-of-ninja-classes-why-kids-love-this-new-fitness-trend/  
[4] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “A Parent’s Guide to Supporting Your Child’s Gymnastics Dreams.” https://goldmedalgymnasticscenter.com/a-parents-guide-to-supporting-your-childs-gymnastics-dreams/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the same provider (Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers), so both pass the DEEP logic/entity check. On WIDTH and checklist coverage, both covered early childhood physical and social-emotional benefits, Ninja class growth and benefits, and competitive-parent support themes adequately. However, Agent A’s USAG levels summary lacks key structural specificity (no clear breakdown of Developmental 1–3, Compulsory 4–5, Optional 6–10, and limited mention of progression criteria). Agent B more clearly references developmental vs. compulsory vs. optional pathways and explicitly mentions skill mastery and evaluation as progression factors, aligning better with the checklist. In terms of presentation, both are well-structured, but Agent B provides slightly clearer thematic framing and more precise structural details. Therefore, Agent B offers a better overall user experience due to stronger completeness on the most technical component (USAG structure), while Agent A’s weakness is a WIDE aggregation gap rather than a logic failure.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Ever wondered how early gymnastics can shape your child’s future? Signing your little one up for gymnastics classes is more than just physical fun. It’s a chance to grow their
rounds.answer_a:
Gold Medal Gymnastics Center (GMGC) publishes a collection of guides that map out a comprehensive developmental pathway for families.

1.  **"Building Blocks for Life: Why Gymnastics is the Best Sport for Young Children"** focuses on early childhood development. It highlights foundational physical benefits like coordination, balance, and kinesthetic awareness—termed the "ABCs of athleticism." It also emphasizes the development of crucial social and emotional skills, such as listening, following directions, and building confidence by mastering new physical tasks in a structured class environment [1].

2.  **"USA Gymnastics Levels Explained 2024: A Parent’s Guide"** serves as a roadmap to the formal competitive system. Its primary focus is to clarify the USA Gymnastics (USAG) structure for parents. It differentiates between the highly structured Development Program (DP), a linear path from Level 1 to 10 for dedicated athletes, and the more flexible Xcel Program. The guide outlines the progression toward the Elite Program, which is for gymnasts aspiring to national and international competition [2].

3.  **"The Rise of Ninja: More Than Just a Class, It’s a Movement"** explains a popular alternative to traditional gymnastics. It highlights how Ninja training combines gymnastics, martial arts, and obstacle courses to develop "athletic intelligence" and physical adaptability. The guide promotes the program's ability to build mental discipline and confidence in a high-energy, engaging format that serves as both a recreational outlet and a cross-training tool [3].

4.  **"A Parent's Guide to Your Child's Competitive Journey"** supports families navigating the demands of competitive gymnastics. It focuses on the crucial roles of the parent, athlete, and coach—the "Trifecta." The guide offers practical and emotional strategies for managing the significant time commitment and helping children cope with the pressures of competition [4].

Considered together, these resources provide a continuous pathway. "Building Blocks" establishes gymnastics as an ideal starting point for any child [1]. From there, the "Ninja" guide presents a dynamic recreational option [3], while the "USA Gymnastics Levels Explained" guide charts the course for those ready for a competitive commitment [2]. Finally, "A Parent's Guide" equips families with the knowledge to successfully support an athlete on that demanding journey, completing the progression from initial enrollment to advanced training [4].

## References
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (2024, May 1). *Building Blocks for Life: Why Gymnastics is the Best Sport for Young Children*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/blog/building-blocks-for-life-why-gymnastics-is-the-best-sport-for-young-children.
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (2024, April 1). *USA Gymnastics Levels Explained 2024: A Parent’s Guide*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/blog/usa-gymnastics-levels-explained-2024-a-parents-guide.
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (2024, February 1). *The Rise of Ninja: More Than Just a Class, It’s a Movement*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/blog/the-rise-of-ninja-more-than-just-a-class-its-a-movement.
[4] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center. (2020, December 1). *A Parent's Guide to Your Child's Competitive Journey*. Retrieved from gmgc.com/blog/a-parents-guide-to-your-childs-competitive-journey.
rounds.answer_b:
Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers publishes this set of parent-oriented resources, and together they map a clear progression from introductory participation to advanced competitive development. In **“Understanding the 2024 USAG Levels in Gymnastics,”** the gym explains the USA Gymnastics pathway from developmental stages through compulsory and optional levels and ultimately toward **Elite** competition [1]. Its primary focus is helping families understand how gymnasts advance, what distinguishes each level, and why evaluation and skill mastery determine placement. The article emphasizes competitive structure, progression, and the increasing technical and training demands associated with higher levels [1].

In **“The Benefits of Enrolling Your Child in Gymnastics Classes at a Young Age,”** Gold Medal focuses on why early participation is valuable beyond athletics alone [2]. It highlights physical benefits such as **strength, flexibility, balance, coordination, and motor-skill development**, while also stressing social and emotional gains including **confidence, discipline, listening skills, and positive peer interaction** [2]. This article frames gymnastics as a foundation for whole-child development and a strong entry point for young beginners [2].

In **“The Rise of Ninja Classes: Why Kids Love This New Fitness Trend,”** the organization describes Ninja-style training as a fast-growing, high-energy alternative or complement to traditional gymnastics [3]. The piece highlights skills and benefits including **agility, strength, coordination, obstacle-navigation ability, and confidence**, while also emphasizing fun, engagement, and broad appeal for children who enjoy movement-based challenges [3]. This positions Ninja classes as another accessible pathway into structured physical training, especially for recreational participants [3].

In **“A Parent’s Guide to Supporting Your Child’s Gymnastics Dreams,”** Gold Medal addresses families whose children are entering or progressing through competitive gymnastics [4]. The guide focuses on parental support strategies, including fostering **healthy motivation**, maintaining **communication with coaches**, balancing **school and training**, and protecting the athlete’s **nutrition, rest, and emotional well-being** [4].

Taken together, these four resources form a comprehensive developmental pathway: early gymnastics builds foundational physical and social skills, Ninja classes broaden engagement and athleticism, the USAG guide explains formal competitive advancement, and the parent guide helps families sustain athletes through the demands of competitive and potentially elite gymnastics [1][2][3][4].

## References
[1] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “Understanding the 2024 USAG Levels in Gymnastics.” https://goldmedalgymnasticscenter.com/understanding-the-2024-usag-levels-in-gymnastics/  
[2] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “The Benefits of Enrolling Your Child in Gymnastics Classes at a Young Age.” https://goldmedalgymnasticscenter.com/the-benefits-of-enrolling-your-child-in-gymnastics-classes-at-a-young-age/  
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “The Rise of Ninja Classes: Why Kids Love This New Fitness Trend.” https://goldmedalgymnasticscenter.com/the-rise-of-ninja-classes-why-kids-love-this-new-fitness-trend/  
[4] Gold Medal Gymnastics Centers, “A Parent’s Guide to Supporting Your Child’s Gymnastics Dreams.” https://goldmedalgymnasticscenter.com/a-parents-guide-to-supporting-your-childs-gymnastics-dreams/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified Gold Medal Gymnastics as the publishing organization, so both pass the Deep Logic (core entity) check. On Width/Completeness, both covered all four required resources and explained their primary focus and developmental benefits. However, Agent B more explicitly referenced the progression through developmental, compulsory, optional, and Elite levels, aligning more closely with the checklist language. Agent A mentioned the Development Program (Level 1–10) and Elite but did not clearly break out compulsory vs. optional levels and introduced Xcel, which was not required. Agent B also provided slightly richer detail in the parent guide (nutrition, rest, emotional well-being) and more explicit articulation of competitive progression. In terms of formatting and user experience, both were clear and well-structured, but Agent B’s presentation was slightly more polished and checklist-aligned. Therefore, Agent B wins on completeness and alignment with the required depth, though both responses are strong.
